title: Ezekiel 45
subtitle: The Sacred District, Just Measures, and Festival Offerings

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Temple Vision

sections.header: Verses 1-8 — The Sacred Portion of the Land

heb.paragraph: The sacred district is called a terumah, a term normally used for the portion 'lifted off' from produce as an offering to YHWH (cf. Num 18:8-19). Here it designates an entire territorial zone dedicated to sacred use. The terumah measures 25,000 by 20,000 cubits (approximately 13 by 10.5 km) and is divided into three east-west strips: one for the sanctuary and Zadokite priests, one for the Levites, and one for the city. This spatial arrangement ensures concentric holiness radiating from the sanctuary.

The nasi receives land on both sides of the sacred district, east and west. The placement of the princely territory flanking the sacred zone rather than enclosing it prevents the pre-exilic pattern of royal encroachment on sacred space. The nasi's land is generous but definitively separated from the holy precinct.

refs.note: The Levitical cities provided the Levites with residential territory throughout the tribal allotments. Ezekiel's plan consolidates Levitical territory into a single district adjacent to the sanctuary.
The condemnation of royal tombs and the 'threshold beside my threshold.' The sacred district's design in chapter 45 architecturally prevents this problem from recurring.
The detailed allotment of the sacred district is elaborated further in chapter 48, where the same territorial structure is described in the context of the full tribal distribution.

notes.note: The sacred district places the sanctuary at the geographic center of the nation. God's dwelling is the gravitational center around which all national life orbits. The Zadokite residential zone surrounding the temple creates a buffer of consecrated persons between the holy place and the common.

The Levites receive a parallel strip of land, equal in width to the priestly zone. Their proximity to the temple without direct altar access visually represents their subordinate but honorable role. The city strip belongs to all Israel, not to any single tribe, making the capital city a truly national possession.

The prince's territory flanks the sacred district rather than surrounding it. This architectural decision embodies the lesson of Israel's history: royal power must serve alongside, not dominate, sacred space. YHWH commands that the princes shall no longer oppress, directly addressing the monarchic abuses cataloged in chapters 22 and 34.
The allotment by lot signifies divine sovereignty in land distribution. The lot eliminates human favoritism and establishes YHWH's direct authority over territorial allocation.

Calvin reads the sacred district as a figure for the principle that God must be given the first and best portion of everything. The territorial arrangement symbolizes the priority of worship in national life. Calvin warns against reading this as a literal land-surveying plan, insisting that the vision communicates spiritual principles through spatial imagery. The prohibition of princely oppression (v. 8) applies to all civil authorities in every age.

The NET notes that the dimensions are given in cubits (not rods), and the total sacred district encompasses approximately 64 square kilometers. The phrase 'when you allot the land as an inheritance' (be-happilkhem et ha-arets be-nachalah) assumes a future redistribution of the entire promised land.

The NET observes that the divine prohibition 'my princes shall no longer oppress my people' uses the verb yanah ('oppress'), the same verb used in Ezekiel 22:7, 29 to describe pre-exilic royal injustice. The territorial arrangement is designed to prevent systemic oppression by spatial separation of political and sacred power.

Block identifies this passage as a 'charter of sacred geography' that reorganizes the entire land according to the principle of concentric holiness. The sanctuary stands at the center, surrounded by priests, then Levites, then the city, then the prince. Block emphasizes that this is not merely urban planning but a theological statement: sacred space generates and orders all other space. The prohibition of princely oppression in v. 8 forms an inclusio with the indictment of rulers in 34:1-10.

Zimmerli classifies this unit as Landverteilungsordnung (land distribution ordinance) and notes its programmatic character: the sacred district establishes the spatial framework for all subsequent legislation in chapters 45-48. He identifies tensions between this passage and chapter 48's fuller description, suggesting editorial development. The terumah concept adapts cultic offering language to territorial allocation, sacralizing geography itself.

hist.context: When the land is divided by lot, a sacred portion (terumah) must be set apart for YHWH. This district measures 25,000 by 20,000 cubits and occupies the geographic center of the redistributed land. It is divided into three horizontal strips: the northern strip (25,000 x 10,000) contains the sanctuary at its center and houses the Zadokite priests; the middle strip (25,000 x 10,000) is assigned to the Levites for their service; the southern strip (25,000 x 5,000) belongs to the city and is open to all Israel. The prince's territory flanks the sacred district to the east and west, extending to the national borders. This arrangement eliminates the pre-exilic problem of royal territory encroaching on sacred space (the 'threshold beside my threshold' of 43:8). YHWH declares that the princes of Israel shall no longer oppress the people but shall give the land to Israel according to its tribes.

heb.paragraph: The demand for accurate weights and measures uses the construct chain 'balances of righteousness' (tsedeq), elevating commercial honesty to the level of covenantal obligation. The ephah (dry measure, approximately 22 liters) and the bath (liquid measure, approximately 22 liters) are standardized as one-tenth of a homer. The shekel is fixed at twenty gerahs, and the mina at sixty shekels. These standards prevent the commercial fraud that the prophets consistently condemned (Amos 8:5; Mic 6:10-11).

refs.note: The Holiness Code demands just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin. Ezekiel extends this principle into the eschatological temple order.
Amos condemns merchants who make the ephah small and the shekel great and cheat with dishonest scales. Ezekiel's standardization aims to prevent precisely this fraud.
A false balance is an abomination to the LORD, but a just weight is His delight. Ezekiel's legislation institutionalizes this proverb.

notes.note: The princes are commanded to put away violence and destruction. The same leaders addressed in chapter 34 (the shepherds who exploited the flock) are now given a positive mandate: practice justice and righteousness. Reform begins at the top.

Just weights and measures are not merely economic regulations but expressions of God's character. A God who is precise in His standards demands precision in His people's dealings. The standardization of the ephah, bath, shekel, and mina creates a unified system that prevents regional variations used to exploit the poor.

Calvin sees this unit as demonstrating that worship and ethics are inseparable. One cannot offer pure sacrifice while cheating in the marketplace. The princes bear particular responsibility because their power enables systemic injustice. Calvin applies this directly to civil magistrates: rulers are accountable to God for the economic justice of their domains.

The NET notes that the equation of the ephah and the bath as equal (both one-tenth of a homer) provides the first explicit statement of this equivalence in the Hebrew Bible, standardizing dry and liquid measurement.

The NET observes that the mina calculation (20 + 25 + 15 = 60 shekels) is unusual. Some LXX manuscripts read 50 shekels, which is the Mesopotamian standard. The MT's 60-shekel mina may reflect a different weighing tradition or a textual corruption.

Block notes the striking placement of commercial legislation within a temple vision. The integration of marketplace ethics into sacred law reflects Ezekiel's priestly conviction that holiness encompasses all of life, not merely the cult. The demand for just weights addresses the very sins that led to the exile: the exploitation of the poor by the powerful (22:29).

Zimmerli identifies this unit as a Mahnwort (admonitory word) directed at the nasi. Its placement between territorial and cultic legislation underscores the prophetic insistence that social justice and proper worship belong to the same divine order. The metrological details may reflect administrative reforms of the Persian period, suggesting post-exilic editorial updating of Ezekiel's vision.

hist.context: Sandwiched between the sacred district legislation and the festival calendar is a brief but significant unit on commercial justice. YHWH commands the princes of Israel to abandon violence and destruction and practice justice and righteousness. The passage standardizes four key measurements: the ephah (dry capacity), the bath (liquid capacity), the shekel (weight), and the mina (larger weight unit). All are anchored to the homer as the base unit: both the ephah and the bath equal one-tenth of a homer. The shekel is twenty gerahs, and the mina is sixty shekels. This legislation addresses a persistent prophetic complaint: merchants cheated customers by using dishonest weights and measures. By standardizing these units in the context of temple legislation, Ezekiel integrates economic ethics into the sacred order. Justice in the marketplace is as essential to YHWH's new order as correct sacrifice in the temple.

sections.header: Verses 13-25 — Offerings and the Festival Calendar

heb.paragraph: The chattat (sin/purification offering) dominates the festival calendar. On the first day of the first month and again on the seventh day, a bull is offered as a chattat to purge the sanctuary. The Passover and the festival of the seventh month each require daily chattat offerings. The chattat functions primarily to remove impurity from sacred space rather than to atone for individual sin, maintaining the sanctuary's fitness for divine habitation.

refs.note: The institution of Passover. Ezekiel's Passover legislation modifies the original by making the prince responsible for providing the offerings and by specifying a seven-day period of daily burnt offerings far exceeding the Mosaic requirements.
The complete Mosaic festival calendar. Ezekiel's calendar notably omits the Day of Atonement, Pentecost, and the Feast of Trumpets, raising questions about the relationship between the two systems.
The detailed offering schedule for each festival. Ezekiel's offerings differ in both quantity and type from the Numbers prescriptions, suggesting an independent or reformulated tradition.

notes.note: The regular offerings are specified in precise quantities. The prince bears the responsibility for providing the offerings for all festivals, Sabbaths, and new moons. This is not optional generosity but obligatory leadership in worship. The leader's primary duty is to facilitate the nation's worship.

The sanctuary purification on the first and seventh days of the first month cleanses the temple for the year's worship. Blood is applied to the doorposts, altar corners, and gate posts — the same three-point application that echoes the original Passover blood on the doorframe (Exod 12:7). The sanctuary must be pure before any festival observance can proceed.

The Passover and the seventh-month festival are the two anchor observances in Ezekiel's calendar. The notable absence of the Day of Atonement may indicate that in the eschatological temple, with God's permanent presence, the annual day of purgation is no longer necessary — or it may be absorbed into the regular purification rituals described in vv. 18-20.

The prince provides the sin offering on behalf of himself and all the people. This mediatorial role is distinct from the priestly function: the prince provides, the priests officiate. The nasi is the patron of worship, not its minister.

Calvin notes the differences between Ezekiel's calendar and the Mosaic legislation without alarm, arguing that the vision presents worship principles rather than a superseding code. The omission of the Day of Atonement is theologically significant for Calvin: in Christ, the once-for-all atonement has been accomplished, making annual repetition unnecessary. The vision points forward to this fulfillment.

The prince's role as provider of offerings for 'himself and all the people' places him in a representative position that for Calvin foreshadows Christ as the one who provides the ultimate offering on behalf of all.

The NET notes that the purification of the sanctuary on the first and seventh days of the first month has no parallel in the Mosaic code. The seven-day interval suggests a thorough bipartite cleansing. Some scholars emend 'seventh day' to 'first day of the seventh month' to create a semiannual purification.

The NET observes that Ezekiel's Passover is described as a 'festival of seven days' with unleavened bread, combining elements that are separated in the Pentateuchal traditions (Passover on the 14th, Unleavened Bread on the 15th-21st).

Block identifies this section as a 'princely worship charter' that systematically outlines the nasi's liturgical obligations. He notes that the prince functions as a patron-benefactor of the cult, providing sacrificial animals and grain at his own expense. This is not royal prerogative but royal obligation. Block highlights the absence of the Day of Atonement as theologically deliberate: in a temple where YHWH's glory has permanently returned and holiness pervades the mountaintop (43:12), the annual crisis of potential divine abandonment that the Day of Atonement addressed no longer obtains.

Zimmerli notes the significant divergences between this calendar and the Priestly calendar of Numbers 28-29 and Leviticus 23. He attributes the differences to Ezekiel's independent priestly tradition, which may predate the final form of P. The omission of the Day of Atonement is, for Zimmerli, evidence that Yom Kippur as described in Leviticus 16 may be a later priestly development not yet known to Ezekiel's tradents. This raises complex questions about the relative dating of P and Ezekiel.

hist.context: The festival calendar outlines the required offerings for the major observances. The first month begins with sanctuary purification on the first and seventh days, using bull blood applied to the doorposts of the temple, the four corners of the altar ledge, and the posts of the inner court gate. Passover begins on the fourteenth day of the first month, with seven days of unleavened bread. During the festival, the prince provides a bull as a sin offering on the first day and a male goat daily for seven days, along with burnt offerings of seven bulls and seven rams daily, with a grain offering of one ephah per bull and one ephah per ram, plus a hin of oil per ephah. The festival of the seventh month (corresponding to the Feast of Tabernacles) follows the same pattern. Notable omissions from the Mosaic calendar include the Feast of Weeks (Pentecost), the Day of Atonement, and the Festival of Trumpets. These omissions have generated extensive scholarly discussion about whether the calendar represents a simplified ideal, a different tradition, or a deliberate theological statement.

themes.note: Worship and Holiness remain dominant as the chapter details the cultic calendar and sacred geography. Justice is notably elevated by the weights-and-measures legislation and the anti-oppression mandate. The integration of economic justice into temple law underscores that holiness extends beyond the cult.

lit.note: Chapter 45 weaves together geography, economics, and liturgy into a unified sacred order. Land, commerce, and worship are not separate domains but interconnected expressions of holiness.

hebtext.note: The sacred district is called a terumah, a term normally used for the portion 'lifted off' from produce as an offering to YHWH (cf. Num 18:8-19). Here it designates an entire territorial zone dedicated to sacred use. The terumah measures 25,000 by 20,000 cubits (approximately 13 by 10.5 km) and is divided into three east-west strips: one for the sanctuary and Zadokite priests, one for the Levites, and one for the city. This spatial arrangement ensures concentric holiness radiating from the sanctuary.

The nasi receives land on both sides of the sacred district, east and west. The placement of the princely territory flanking the sacred zone rather than enclosing it prevents the pre-exilic pattern of royal encroachment on sacred space. The nasi's land is generous but definitively separated from the holy precinct.

The demand for accurate weights and measures uses the construct chain 'balances of righteousness' (tsedeq), elevating commercial honesty to the level of covenantal obligation. The ephah (dry measure, approximately 22 liters) and the bath (liquid measure, approximately 22 liters) are standardized as one-tenth of a homer. The shekel is fixed at twenty gerahs, and the mina at sixty shekels. These standards prevent the commercial fraud that the prophets consistently condemned (Amos 8:5; Mic 6:10-11).

The chattat (sin/purification offering) dominates the festival calendar. On the first day of the first month and again on the seventh day, a bull is offered as a chattat to purge the sanctuary. The Passover and the festival of the seventh month each require daily chattat offerings. The chattat functions primarily to remove impurity from sacred space rather than to atone for individual sin, maintaining the sanctuary's fitness for divine habitation.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: The NET observes that the divine prohibition 'my princes shall no longer oppress my people' uses the verb yanah ('oppress'), the same verb used in Ezekiel 22:7, 29 to describe pre-exilic royal injustice…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Sacred District, Just Measures, and Festival Offerings

translations.text: “ ‘You are to give the city as its property an area 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, adjoining the sacred portion; it will belong to all Israel.
“Alongside the portion set apart as the holy district you shall assign for the property of the city an area 5,000 cubits broad and 25,000 cubits long. It shall belong to the whole house of Israel.

“ ‘This is what the Sovereign Lord says: You have gone far enough, princes of Israel! Give up your violence and oppression and do what is just and right. Stop dispossessing my people, declares the Sovereign Lord.
“Thus says the Lord GOD: Enough, O princes of Israel! Put away violence and oppression, and execute justice and righteousness. Cease your evictions of my people, declares the Lord GOD.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Sacred District, Just Measures, and Festival Offerings within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
